name: Huda Kattan
title: The Blogger Who Turned Eyelashes Into Empire
dek: Huda Kattan went from finance refugee to billion-dollar beauty mogul, one false lash at a time.
body_html:
Before the word influencer meant anything, before the algorithm decided who got famous, Huda Kattan was a finance grad in Dubai who quit her job because spreadsheets were slowly liquefying her soul. She picked up a makeup brush instead. Roughly a billion dollars later, the spreadsheets are looking pretty foolish.

The Blog Before the Boom

Kattan was born in Oklahoma to Iraqi parents, studied finance, and worked the kind of corporate gig that pays the bills and dims the lights inside you. So she trained in makeup artistry in Los Angeles, moved to Dubai, and started a humble little blog called Huda Beauty in 2010, dishing tutorials and brutally honest product reviews.

The blog blew up because Kattan was the opposite of polished-and-fake. She was funny, generous with secrets, and obsessed with the details. As her audience swelled into the millions, she spotted a gap she could fill herself: false eyelashes that actually looked good and didn't fall apart. With seed money reportedly borrowed from her sister, she launched her own lash line.

The Lashes Heard Round the World

One early believer? Kim Kardashian wore the lashes, the internet noticed, and the rest detonated. The first batch sold out almost instantly. Kattan had done something rare: she'd built the audience first, then handed them a product they already trusted because they trusted her.

She didn't buy an audience to sell a product. She built a community and let it tell her what to make.

That's the creative masterstroke. Every launch became a conversation with her followers, not a broadcast at them. The branding leaned hot pink, bold, and unapologetically maximalist, a visual scream of confidence on every shelf and feed. Huda Beauty expanded from lashes into liquid lipsticks, the cult-favorite Desert Dusk palette, foundations, and skincare under the Wishful label, plus the fragrance brand Kayali with her sister Mona.

From Bedroom Blog to Billion-Dollar Beauty House

The numbers got serious. A private-equity investment reportedly valued Huda Beauty north of a billion dollars, making Kattan one of the wealthiest self-made women in beauty and a fixture on rich lists. She later made headlines again by buying back outside investor stakes to reclaim full control, a power move that doubled as a statement about founders keeping the keys to their own kingdom.

What's wild is how thoroughly she rewrote the playbook. She turned social media from a marketing channel into the actual product-development engine, treating millions of followers as a focus group, a hype machine, and a sales force rolled into one. Brands now spend fortunes trying to manufacture the authenticity Kattan just... had.

She remains the face, the voice, and the gut instinct of the whole operation, still posting, still testing, still calling out a bad mascara when she sees one. She's also gotten candid about the loneliness and pressure of running an empire built on her own face, which only deepens the bond with an audience that grew up alongside the brand.

The empire works because the human at its center never stopped being a fan first. Where legacy beauty houses spent decades guarding their secrets, Kattan gave hers away for free and got an army in return. That's the whole magic trick. Build the trust, and the billion-dollar shelf builds itself.
